member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IClass.Fields
member.summary: The fields collection for this object class.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: M:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IClass.FindField(System.String)
member.summary: The index of the field with the specified name.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IClass.HasOID
member.summary: Indicates if the class has an object identity (OID) field.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IClass.Indexes
member.summary: The indexes collection for this object class.
member.remarks: The Indexes object represents a collection of indexes on a table. Indexes are designed to improve access times to data. The Indexes collection object operates in a very similar way to the Fields collection object.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ICursor
member.summary: Provides access to members that hand out enumerated rows, field collections and allows for the updating, deleting and inserting of rows.

member.summary: Delete the existing Row in the database corresponding to the current position of the cursor.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ICursor.Fields
member.summary: The Fields Collection for this cursor.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.summary: Flush any outstanding buffered writes to the database.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IDataset.Workspace
member.summary: The workspace containing this dataset.
member.remarks:
A Workspace is a container of spatial and non-spatial datasets such as feature classes, raster datasets, and tables. It provides methods to instantiate existing datasets and to create new datasets. Workspaces are classified into types specified by the esriWorkspaceType enumerator: FileSystemWorkspace, LocalDatabaseWorkspace, and RemoteDatabaseWorkspace. Shapefiles and ArcGIS for Desktop Advanced workspaces are examples of FileSystemWorkspace. A personal geodatabase stored in an Access or a File Geodatabase is an example of a LocalDatabaseWorkspace. A geodatabase stored in an RDBMS such as Oracle, DB2, SQL Server, or Informix and accessed via ArcSDE is an example of a RemoteDatabaseWorkspace.

 

A Workspace hands out a WorkspaceName object as the value of its FullName property. For example, the WorkspaceName for a workspace can be persisted in a map document. An application can call the Open() method on the workspace name after loading it from persistent storage to connect to and get an object reference to the workspace.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IDENetworkDataset.ConfigurationKeyword
member.summary: The database configuration keyword for the network dataset.
member.remarks:
Configuration keywords can be of two types:

  • esriConfigurationKeywordGeneral, which references storage parameters for creating feature classes and tables
  • esriConfigurationKeywordNetwork, which references storage parameters for creating geometric networks or network datasets
  • esriConfigurationKeywordTopology, which references storage parameters for creating topologies

An enumeration of ConfigurationKeyword objects for an ArcSDE or File geodatabase workspace is returned by the IWorkspaceConfiguration interface.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IEvaluatedNetworkAttribute.DefaultEvaluator(ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriNetworkElementType)
member.summary: Evaluator used to derive attribute values for the given element type when an evaluator is not specified for a network element's source.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IEvaluatedNetworkAttribute.DENetworkDataset
member.summary: Evaluator used to derive values from the given network source for this network attribute.
member.remarks: Access the DENetworkDataset for an existing INetworkDataset by calling IDatasetComponent::DataElement.

example.code:
IDENetworkDataset GetDataElementFromNetworkDataset(INetworkDataset networkDataset)
{
  IDatasetComponent datasetComponent = networkDataset as IDatasetComponent;
  return (datasetComponent.DataElement as IDENetworkDataset);
}

member.summary: Evaluator used to derive values from the given network source for this network attribute.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IExternalSerializerGdb
member.summary: Provides high-level serialization methods for AMF and JSON formats.
Convenience method. Creates and returns error object as byte SAFEARRAY.
Writes error object.
Writes geometry object.
Writes feature recordset.
Writes feature row object
Writes spatial reference object. Pass NULL as propname to write standalone JSON object.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IExternalSerializerGdb2
member.summary: Provides high-level serialization methods for AMF and JSON formats.
Convenience method. Creates and returns error object as byte SAFEARRAY.
Obtains the Quantization Transform JSON Ojbect setup during writer install.
Writes error object.
Writes geometry object.
Writes feature recordset.
Writes feature recordset and returns number of records serialized.
Writes feature recordset for both JSON and GeoJSON format and returns number of records serialized.
Writes feature recordset for both JSON and GeoJSON format with quantization support and returns number of records serialized.

Writes feature recordset for both JSON and GeoJSON format with quantization support and returns number of records serialized, for other use cases like Map Server end, we still need to report crs even GeoJSON no longer has it.
Writes feature row object
Writes spatial reference object. Pass NULL as propname to write standalone JSON object.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IFeatureCursor.Fields
member.summary: The fields Collection for this cursor.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IField.GeometryDef
member.summary: The geometry definition for the field if IsGeometry is TRUE.
member.remarks:
The GeometryDef object defines the spatial qualities of a feature class. The most fundmental spatial quality is the Geometry type, for example, point, line, and polygon. Other information necessary to define the feature class includes the spatial referencing system, whether the vertices have height or measure data, and, for geodatabases, the spatial index parameters.

The GeometryDef can be accessed from a shape field, that is a field of type esriFieldTypeGeometry. The actual geometry type is defined by the esriGeometryType enumeration, though currently only four values are acceptable with respect to GeometryDef objects: esriGeometryPoint, esriGeometryMultipoint, esriGeometryPolyline, and esriGeometryPolygon.

Beware of the confusion between the esriGeometryType enumeration and the similarly named esriFeatureType and esriShapeType. The following table shows the different roles of those enumerations.

ArcObjects Enumeration Values Interfaces that use the enumeration
esriGeometryType

Type of geometry in a geodatabase. Those in bold are valid for feature classes, others are component geometry objects.		 esriGeometryPoint
esriGeometryMultipoint
esriGeometryPolygon
esriGeometryPolyline
esriGeometryLine
esriGeometryCircularArc
esriGeometryEllipticArc
esriGeometryBezier3Curve
esriGeometryPath
esriGeometryNull
esriGeometryRing
esriGeometryEnvelope
esriGeometryAny
esriGeometryBag
esriGeometryMultiPatch
esriGeometryTriangleStrip
esriGeometryTriangleFan
esriGeometryRay
esriGeometrySphere
esriGeometryTriangles

IGeometry::GeometryType
IGeometryDef::GeometryType

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IFieldEdit.GeometryDef_2
member.summary: The geometry definition if IsGeometry is TRUE.
member.remarks:
The GeometryDef object defines the spatial qualities of a feature class. The most fundmental spatial quality is the Geometry type, for example, point, line, and polygon. Other information necessary to define the feature class includes the spatial referencing system, whether the vertices have height or measure data, and, for geodatabases, the spatial index parameters.

The GeometryDef can be accessed from a shape field, that is a field of type esriFieldTypeGeometry. The actual geometry type is defined by the esriGeometryType enumeration, though currently only four values are acceptable with respect to GeometryDef objects: esriGeometryPoint, esriGeometryMultipoint, esriGeometryPolyline, and esriGeometryPolygon.

Beware of the confusion between the esriGeometryType enumeration and the similarly named esriFeatureType and esriShapeType. The following table shows the different roles of those enumerations.

ArcObjects Enumeration Values Interfaces that use the enumeration
esriGeometryType

Type of geometry in a geodatabase. Those in bold are valid for feature classes, others are component geometry objects.		 esriGeometryPoint
esriGeometryMultipoint
esriGeometryPolygon
esriGeometryPolyline
esriGeometryLine
esriGeometryCircularArc
esriGeometryEllipticArc
esriGeometryBezier3Curve
esriGeometryPath
esriGeometryNull
esriGeometryRing
esriGeometryEnvelope
esriGeometryAny
esriGeometryBag
esriGeometryMultiPatch
esriGeometryTriangleStrip
esriGeometryTriangleFan
esriGeometryRay
esriGeometrySphere
esriGeometryTriangles

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IIndex
member.summary: Provides access to members that return information about the index.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IIndex.Fields
member.summary: The fields collection for this index.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IIndex.IsAscending
member.summary: Indicates if the index is based on ascending order.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IIndex.IsUnique
member.summary: Indicates if the index is unique.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IIndex.Name
member.summary: The name of the index.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IJSONConverterGdb
member.summary: Provides high-level deserialization methods for JSON.
Query domain info from IDomain and populate IJSONObject.
Query domain info from IDomain and populate IJSONObject. If returnDomainNames is true, it returns only domain name.
Query domain info from IDomain and populate IJSONObject.If fieldDomain is equal to typeDomain, it returns the domain as inherited.
Query subtype info from subtype and populate IJSONObject.
Reads RecordSet object from IJSONReader.

Reads RecordSet object from IJSONObject. pDefaultSchema is used only when input JSON has no 'fields' attribute. Pass NULL if not needed. Also, sometimes field names are corrected by the GeoDatabase, use ppOriginalToNewFieldMap to find new field names.
Writes RecordSet to IJSONWriter. Writes closing bracked if bWriteEndOfObj is VARIANT_TRUE. Set bWriteEndOfObj to VARIANT_FALSE if you want to append additional JSON before closing object.

member.summary: Provides access to members that control the Linear Dispatch layout.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.AbsoluteUnit
member.summary: Indicates whether the values set for the distance parameters are interpreted as absolute units.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.ExpandLeaves
member.summary: Indicates whether leaf junctionsâ€”that is, junctions with one connectionâ€”can be expanded during the algorithm execution.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.JunctionsPlacement
member.summary: Indicates the type of junction placement that is applied during the layout execution.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.LeavesShift
member.summary: Indicates the maximum distance the leaf junctions is spaced from the junctions to which they connect when they are authorized to be moved during the layout execution.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.MaximumShift
member.summary: Indicates the maximum distance the junctions with two connections is spaced from their edge extremity after the layout execution. This parameter is only used when JunctionsPlacement type is esriLayoutIterativeDistance.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.MinimumShift
member.summary: Indicates the minimum distance the junctions with two connections is spaced from their edge extremity after the layout execution. This parameter is only used when JunctionsPlacement type is esriLayoutUserDefineDistance.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.MoveLeaves
member.summary: Indicates whether leaf junctionsâ€”that is, junctions with one connectionâ€”can be moved during the algorithm execution.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.NumberOfIterations
member.summary: Indicates the number of Linear Dispatch layout iterations that is executed before the final result is shown. This parameter is only used when JunctionsPlacement is esriLayoutIterativeDistance.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ILinearDispatchLayout.PreservePath
member.summary: Indicates whether the layout algorithm preserves the vertices along the diagram edges related to the moved diagram junctions when executing.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout
member.summary: Provides access to members that control the Mainline Tree layout.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.AbsoluteUnit
member.summary: Indicates whether the values set for the spacing parameters are interpreted as absolute units.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.AlongSpacing
member.summary: Indicates the spacing between the diagram junctions that display along the main line direction; that is, spacing between a diagram junction belonging to the (n) hierarchical level and a diagram junction belonging to the (n+1) hierarchical level.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.BreakPointPosition
member.summary: Percentage number that indicates the relative position of the break point on the last segment of the diagram edges; that is on the diagram edge segment parallel to the main line.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.Direction
member.summary: Indicates the direction of the main line.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.DisjoinedGraphSpacing
member.summary: Indicates the minimum spacing that must separate diagram features belonging to disjoined graphs when the diagram contains such graphs.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.EdgesType
member.summary: Indicates the type of display for the diagram edges (right angles, curved geometry, or no refinement).

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.Offset
member.summary: Indicates the distance used to separate diagram edge segments that are overlapping. This parameter is only used when EdgesType is esriLayoutOrthogonalEdge.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.PerpendicularSpacing
member.summary: Indicates the spacing between the diagram junctions that display perpendicularly to the main line direction; that is, spacing between the diagram junctions that belong to a same hierarchical level.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainLineTreeLayout.Placement
member.summary: Indicates how branches from the main line is relatively placed with regard to its direction.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainRingLayout.Offset
member.summary: Indicates the distance used to separate diagram edge segments that are overlapping. This parameter is only used when EdgesType is esriLayoutOrthogonalEdge.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IMainRingLayout.RingHeight
member.summary: Indicates the height of the main ring.

Adjusts the value of a network attribute for a junction element.
Adjusts the value of a network attribute for a turn element.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IQueryDescription
member.summary: Query information

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IQueryDescription.Fields
member.summary: The resultset fields.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRecordSet
member.summary: Provides access to the rows or features in a recordset.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRecordSet.Cursor(System.Boolean)
member.summary: A cursor over the rows or features in the recordset.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRecordSet.Fields
member.summary: The fields of the recordset.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRecordSet.IsFeatureCollection
member.summary: Indicates if the recordset is a feature collection.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRecordSet.Table
member.summary: The source table or feature class for the recordset.
member.remarks:
A Table object has one or more columns, referred to as fields, and contains an unordered collection of rows. Each row has for each field exactly one value in the data type of the field.

A Table is a Dataset and properties such as the name of the table, the persistable name object for the table, and the workspace containing the table, may be obtained via the IDataset interface. In relational terms, a Table object represents an RDBMS table or view. In objected-oriented terms, a Table object represents an ObjectClass or a RelationshipClass in a geodatabase. A Table object hands out Row objects which themselves support application callable methods depending on the type of data stored in the table.

The Name property of a Table, accessible via the IDataset interface, returns its fully qualified name. The level of qualification may vary depending on the host DBMS. For example, a table named ‘pipes’, owned by a user named ‘gas’ may be called ‘pipes’ in and Access or File Geodatabase, ‘gas.pipes’ on Oracle and ‘mydb.gas.pipes’ on SQLServer. The ParseTableNamemethod on the ISqlSyntax interface supported by the table’s workspace can be used to split the fully qualified name into its components.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRow.Table
member.summary: The Table for the row.
member.remarks:
A Table object has one or more columns, referred to as fields, and contains an unordered collection of rows. Each row has for each field exactly one value in the data type of the field.

A Table is a Dataset and properties such as the name of the table, the persistable name object for the table, and the workspace containing the table, may be obtained via the IDataset interface. In relational terms, a Table object represents an RDBMS table or view. In objected-oriented terms, a Table object represents an ObjectClass or a RelationshipClass in a geodatabase. A Table object hands out Row objects which themselves support application callable methods depending on the type of data stored in the table.

The Name property of a Table, accessible via the IDataset interface, returns its fully qualified name. The level of qualification may vary depending on the host DBMS. For example, a table named ‘pipes’, owned by a user named ‘gas’ may be called ‘pipes’ in and Access or File Geodatabase, ‘gas.pipes’ on Oracle and ‘mydb.gas.pipes’ on SQLServer. The ParseTableNamemethod on the ISqlSyntax interface supported by the table’s workspace can be used to split the fully qualified name into its components.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRowBuffer
member.summary: Provides access to members used for getting and modifying a rows values and for getting the fields in the row.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IRowBuffer.Fields
member.summary: The fields Collection for this row buffer.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.summary: The value of the field with the specified index.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ISimpleDataConverter
member.summary: Provides access to members used for data conversions.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ISimpleDataConverter.ConfigurationKeyword
member.summary: The configuration keyword for the new FeatureClass.
member.remarks:
Configuration keywords can be of two types:

  • esriConfigurationKeywordGeneral, which references storage parameters for creating feature classes and tables
  • esriConfigurationKeywordNetwork, which references storage parameters for creating geometric networks or network datasets
  • esriConfigurationKeywordTopology, which references storage parameters for creating topologies

An enumeration of ConfigurationKeyword objects for an ArcSDE or File geodatabase workspace is returned by the IWorkspaceConfiguration interface.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ISmartTreeLayout.PerpendicularSpacing

member.summary: Provides access to members that return and modify information to sort a table.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.Fields
member.summary: Comma list of field names to sort on.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.summary: A id by its index value.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.QueryFilter
member.summary: The query filter on table or selection set.

member.summary: Cursor of sorted rows.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.SelectionSet
member.summary: The selection set as a source of the data to sort on. Cancels Cursor.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.SelectionSet
member.summary: The selection set as a source of the data to sort on. Cancels Cursor.
Sort rows.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.SortCharacters(System.String)
member.summary: Number of characters to sort on, for string fields. A null (default) sorts on the whole string.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.Table
member.summary: The table as a source of the data to sort on.
member.remarks:
A Table object has one or more columns, referred to as fields, and contains an unordered collection of rows. Each row has for each field exactly one value in the data type of the field.

A Table is a Dataset and properties such as the name of the table, the persistable name object for the table, and the workspace containing the table, may be obtained via the IDataset interface. In relational terms, a Table object represents an RDBMS table or view. In objected-oriented terms, a Table object represents an ObjectClass or a RelationshipClass in a geodatabase. A Table object hands out Row objects which themselves support application callable methods depending on the type of data stored in the table.

The Name property of a Table, accessible via the IDataset interface, returns its fully qualified name. The level of qualification may vary depending on the host DBMS. For example, a table named ‘pipes’, owned by a user named ‘gas’ may be called ‘pipes’ in and Access or File Geodatabase, ‘gas.pipes’ on Oracle and ‘mydb.gas.pipes’ on SQLServer. The ParseTableNamemethod on the ISqlSyntax interface supported by the table’s workspace can be used to split the fully qualified name into its components.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITableSort.Table
member.summary: The table as a source of the data to sort on.
member.remarks:

In relational terms, a Table object represents an RDBMS table or view. In objected-oriented terms, a Table object represents an ObjectClass or a RelationshipClass in a geodatabase. A Table object hands out Row objects which themselves support application callable methods depending on the type of data stored in the table.

The Name property of a Table, accessible via the IDataset interface, returns its fully qualified name. The level of qualification may vary depending on the host DBMS. member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITin.Fields
member.summary: The collection of attribute items of the TIN.
member.remarks:
Each table in a database has an ordered collection of fields, there is always at least one field in a table. The ordered collection behaves like a list, so it is possible to access individual fields by a numbered position (or index) in the list.

A Fields collection can also exist independently of a table, for example when creating an index on a table you will need to define which fields take part in the index. There are many interfaces which either require or return a Fields collection; these include IClass (therefore also IObjectClass and IFeatureClass), ICursor, IIndex, IFeatureClassDraw, IRowBuffer, ISimpleDataConverter, ITableSort and IValidate.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITopologyErrorFeature.TopologyRule
member.summary: The topology rule that caused the topology error.
member.remarks: The TopologyRule class is used to define the permissible spatial relationships between features within a topology.  Topology rules can be defined with a single feature class or between two feature classes.  Topology rules can also be defined to the subtype level of a feature class.  Topology rules have an origin and a destination feature class, either of which can be set to the subtype level.  Depending on the type of topology rule that is implemented, the destination feature class properties may be irrelevant.

member.name: M:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.ITopologyProtectNames.ObjectClass
member.remarks:
An object class is a table whose rows represent entities, modeled as objects with properties and behavior. The row objects handed out by an object class support the IRow and the IObject interfaces.

An object class may participate in any number of relationship classes that relate its instances to objects (entities) in other object classes.

An object class may contain a discriminating field, referred to as the subtype field that may be used to partition its instances into a number of subtypes. All subtypes share the same field definition and are stored in the same table, however individual subtypes may differ in the default values and domains assigned to fields. The subtyping mechanism can also be used in defining attribute and connectivity rules that apply to the instances of the object class. The subtyping mechanism is a lightweight alternative to creating multiple subclasses each represented by its own object class.

An object class has a non-negative object class ID which is unique within the geodatabase. This ID is assigned to the object class at the time that it is created or at the time that an existing table in the RDBMS is registered with the geodatabase as an object class. The Name of the object class is the same as the name of the table in the DBMS in which the objects in the object class are stored and follows the same fully qualified naming conventions.

An object class may have an AliasName property that is stored as part of its definition by the geodatabase. The AliasName may be retrieved and used for display purposes by applications.

An object class may have a ModelName property that is stored as part of its definition by the geodatabase. The model name is not exposed to end users and if defined, can be used as a search key to find an object class by a standard model name that is adhered to across databases. A model name must be unique across the workspace.

member.summary: Provides access to events that occur on versions in services. This interface is for use in Server and especially Server Object Extensions.

A version was deleted. Event is reported on DEFAULT and the deleted version.
A version was posted. Event is reported on DEFAULT and the posted version.
A version was reconciled. Event is reported on DEFAULT and the reconciled version.
A version was renamed. Event is reported on DEFAULT and the renamed version.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IWorkspace.WorkspaceFactory
member.summary: The factory that created the workspace.
member.remarks: A WorkspaceFactory is a dispenser of workspaces and allows a client to connect to a workspace specified by a set of connection properties. A workspace represents a database or a datasource that contains one or more datasets. Examples of datasets include tables, feature classes and relationship classes. A WorkspaceFactory is a cocreatable, singleton object. A WorkspaceFactory maintains a pool of currently connected, active workspaces that are being referenced by the application. Connection properties are specified using a PropertySet object and can be saved to a connection file. A WorkspaceFactory also supports methods that can be used to browse and manage file system workspaces, and methods to manage connection files for remote database workspaces.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IWorkspaceExtension2.Workspace
member.summary: The workspace associated with this Workspace Extension.
member.remarks:
A Workspace is a container of spatial and non-spatial datasets such as feature classes, raster datasets, and tables. It provides methods to instantiate existing datasets and to create new datasets. Workspaces are classified into types specified by the esriWorkspaceType enumerator: FileSystemWorkspace, LocalDatabaseWorkspace, and RemoteDatabaseWorkspace. Shapefiles and ArcGIS for Desktop Advanced workspaces are examples of FileSystemWorkspace. A personal geodatabase stored in an Access or a File Geodatabase is an example of a LocalDatabaseWorkspace. A geodatabase stored in an RDBMS such as Oracle, DB2, SQL Server, or Informix and accessed via ArcSDE is an example of a RemoteDatabaseWorkspace.

 

A Workspace hands out a WorkspaceName object as the value of its FullName property. For example, the WorkspaceName for a workspace can be persisted in a map document. An application can call the Open() method on the workspace name after loading it from persistent storage to connect to and get an object reference to the workspace.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IWorkspaceHelper
member.summary: Provides access to members that allow you to get a reference to a Workspace from a Workspace Extension.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IWorkspaceHelper.Workspace
member.summary: The workspace for this workspace helper.
member.remarks:
A Workspace is a container of spatial and non-spatial datasets such as feature classes, raster datasets, and tables. It provides methods to instantiate existing datasets and to create new datasets. Workspaces are classified into types specified by the esriWorkspaceType enumerator: FileSystemWorkspace, LocalDatabaseWorkspace, and RemoteDatabaseWorkspace. Shapefiles and ArcGIS for Desktop Advanced workspaces are examples of FileSystemWorkspace. A personal geodatabase stored in an Access or a File Geodatabase is an example of a LocalDatabaseWorkspace. A geodatabase stored in an RDBMS such as Oracle, DB2, SQL Server, or Informix and accessed via ArcSDE is an example of a RemoteDatabaseWorkspace.

 

A Workspace hands out a WorkspaceName object as the value of its FullName property. For example, the WorkspaceName for a workspace can be persisted in a map document. An application can call the Open() method on the workspace name after loading it from persistent storage to connect to and get an object reference to the workspace.

member.summary: The type of the associated workspace.

member.name: P:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.IWorkspaceName.WorkspaceFactory
member.summary: The workspace factory of the WorkspaceName.
member.remarks: A WorkspaceFactory is a dispenser of workspaces and allows a client to connect to a workspace specified by a set of connection properties. A workspace represents a database or a datasource that contains one or more datasets. Examples of datasets include tables, feature classes and relationship classes. A WorkspaceFactory is a cocreatable, singleton object. A WorkspaceFactory maintains a pool of currently connected, active workspaces that are being referenced by the application. Connection properties are specified using a PropertySet object and can be saved to a connection file. A WorkspaceFactory also supports methods that can be used to browse and manage file system workspaces, and methods to manage connection files for remote database workspaces.

member.summary: 2: Medium degree of freedom. The area used to move the diagram junctions will be moderate.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutDegreeOfFreedom.esriLayoutHighDegree
member.summary: 3: High degree of freedom. The area used to move the diagram junctions will be very large.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutJunctionsPlacement
member.summary: Types of junctions placement for the Linear Dispatch layout.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutJunctionsPlacement.esriLayoutEqualDistance
member.summary: 1: All junctions with two connected edges will be moved so the distances between them and their two connected junctions are equal.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutJunctionsPlacement.esriLayoutUserDefineDistance
member.summary: 2: All junctions with two connected edges will be moved so the specified minimum shift distance is kept between them and the other end of the edges to which they connect.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutJunctionsPlacement.esriLayoutIterativeDistance
member.summary: 3: All junctions with two connected edges will be moved slightly according to the specified number of iterations and maximum shift distance; that is, until the specified number of iterations or maximum distance between the junctions is reached.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMainRingType
member.summary: Types of main ring form for the Main Ring layout.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMainRingType.esriLayoutMainRingRectangle
member.summary: 0: The junctions along the detected main ring will display around a rectangle.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMainRingType.esriLayoutMainRingEllipse
member.summary: 1: The junctions along the detected main ring will display around an ellipse.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMarkCrossingPosition
member.summary: Position of the circular arc that will be placed at each location where diagram edges cross each other at right angles.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMarkCrossingPosition.esriLayoutMarkCrossingLeft
member.summary: 1: The circular arc will be placed to the left of the vertical segment.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMarkCrossingPosition.esriLayoutMarkCrossingRight
member.summary: 2: The circular arc will be placed to the right of the vertical segment.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMarkCrossingPosition.esriLayoutMarkCrossingAbove
member.summary: 3: The circular arc will be placed above the horizontal segment.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutMarkCrossingPosition.esriLayoutMarkCrossingBelow
member.summary: 4: The circular arc will be placed below the horizontal segment.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutNumberOfDirection
member.summary: Number of directions options for the Angle Directed layout.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutNumberOfDirection.esriLayout4Directions
member.summary: 4: Four directions; that is, 90 degrees incremental direction.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutNumberOfDirection.esriLayout8Directions
member.summary: 8: Height directions; that is, 45 degrees incremental direction.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutNumberOfDirection.esriLayout12Directions
member.summary: 12: Twelve directions; that is, 30 degrees incremental direction.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutTreeDirection
member.summary: Directions used for the tree layouts.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutTreeDirection.esriLayoutRightLeft
member.summary: 0: The tree will be horizontally drawn starting from the right and ending to the left.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutTreeDirection.esriLayoutTopDown
member.summary: 1: The tree will be vertically drawn starting from the top and ending to the bottom.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutTreeDirection.esriLayoutLeftRight
member.summary: 2: The tree will be horizontally drawn starting from the left and ending to the right.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutTreeDirection.esriLayoutBottomUp
member.summary: 3: The tree will be vertically drawn starting from the bottom and ending to the top.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutVertexRemovalRule
member.summary: Vertex removal rules for the Compression layout.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutVertexRemovalRule.esriRemoveAllVertices
member.summary: 1: All vertices on all edges will be removed.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutVertexRemovalRule.esriKeepContainedVertices
member.summary: 2: Vertices along edges that are contents are kept and vertices along edges that come out of the container are removed.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutVertexRemovalRule.esriKeepContainedVerticesAndFirstOutside
member.summary: 3: Vertices along edges that are contents are kept as well as the first vertex along edges that come out of the container. Other vertices are removed.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLayoutVertexRemovalRule.esriKeepOnlyVerticesOfContainedEdges
member.summary: 4: Vertices along edges that are contents are kept. Vertices along other edges are removed.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError
member.summary: Error codes for locating operations.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_OK
member.summary: 0: Locating was successful.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_INVALIDRID
member.summary: 1: The route location's route ID is invalid (NULL, empty or invalid value).

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_INVALIDMEASURE
member.summary: 2: At least one of the route location's measure values is invalid.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_CANT_FIND_ROUTE
member.summary: 3: The route does not exist.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_ROUTE_SHAPE_EMPTY
member.summary: 4: The route does not have a shape or the shape is empty.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_CANT_FIND_LOCATION
member.summary: 5: Could not find route location's shape (the route has no M's or the route location's measures don't exist on the route).

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_CANT_FIND_EXTENT
member.summary: 6: Could not find route location's shape, the from-measure and the to-measure are outside of the route measures.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_FROM_PARTIAL_MATCH
member.summary: 7: Could not find the entire route location's shape, the from-measure was outside of the route measure range.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_TO_PARTIAL_MATCH
member.summary: 8: Could not find the entire route location's shape, the to-measure was outside of the route measure range.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_ROUTE_MS_NULL
member.summary: 9: The route does not have Ms or Ms are null.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_ROUTE_NOT_MAWARE
member.summary: 10: The route is not a polyline M aware.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_FROM_TO_PARTIAL_MATCH
member.summary: 11: Could not find the entire route location's shape, the from-measure and the to-measure were outside of the route measure range.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_MULTIPLE_LOCATION
member.summary: 12: More than one point location were found. Measures are probably not unique along the routes.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.esriLocatingError.LOCATING_E_NULL_EXTENT
member.summary: 13: The from-measure is equal to the to-measure.

member.name: T:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes
member.summary: Different methods to perform raster resampling.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_NearestNeighbor
member.summary: 0: Resample pixel by nearest neighbor.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_BilinearInterpolation
member.summary: 1: Resample pixel by bilinear interpolation.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_CubicConvolution
member.summary: 2: Resample pixel by cubic convolution.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_Majority
member.summary: 3: Resample pixel by majority value.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_BilinearInterpolationPlus
member.summary: 4: Resample pixel by bilinear interpolation, requiring all four neighboring pixels.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_BilinearGaussBlur
member.summary: 5: Resample pixel by bilinear interpolation. If downsampling by factor 2 or more, blur source pixel using 2D Gaussian.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_BilinearGaussBlurPlus
member.summary: 6: Same as RSP_BilinearGaussBlur, but requiring all four neighboring pixels.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_Average
member.summary: 7: Resample vector field (two band) pixel by vector average.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_Minimum
member.summary: 8: Resample pixel by average value.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_Maximum
member.summary: 9: Resample pixel by maximum value.

member.name: F:ESRI.ArcGIS.Geodatabase.rstResamplingTypes.RSP_VectorAverage
member.summary: 10: Resample vector field (two band) pixel by vector average.
